title: Quasi-2D trapped tilted dipoles at zero and finite temperatures in the strongly dipolar regime
abstract: Motivated by the recent experimental observation of dipolar supersolid stripes in a quasi two-dimensional geometry [arXiv:2512.13280 (2025)], we study a trapped system of fully polarized dipoles in a strongly axially confined geometry, both at zero and finite temperatures, by means of Bogoliubov theory. The dipoles are strongly harmonically trapped along the z axis and subjected to a box trap in the x-y plane. We characterize the physics of the trapped system at zero and finite temperatures as a function of the tilting angle of the dipoles, the number of particles and the scattering length, restricting ourselves to the experimentally relevant regime of large condensate fractions. We also illustrate the influence of the aspect ratio of the box trap in the liquid character of the system and its structure. We observe a remarkable promotion of spatial modulations when temperature is increased while keeping the total particle number constant for specific configurations, in qualitative agreement with previous Monte Carlo results in a 3D geometry. Our results are useful to understand the zero temperature physics of the trapped dipolar system in the quasi-2D limit and in the strongly dipolar regime. In addition, they allow to assess the effect of temperature in its equilibrium properties in experimentally relevant conditions, which may be useful for thermometry applications.
summary: This theoretical study analyzes trapped dipolar quantum gases confined to a quasi-2D geometry, focusing on the interplay between strong dipole interactions and finite temperature effects. Using Bogoliubov theory, the authors derive an extended Gross-Pitaevskii equation that accounts for thermal fluctuations. The results show how temperature can induce spatial modulations in the density profile, providing insights into supersolid physics and potential thermometry applications.
main_problem: The study investigates the physics of quasi-2D trapped dipolar Bose-Einstein Condensates (BECs) at both zero and finite temperatures in the strongly dipolar regime.
main_result: The research characterizes how increasing temperature can promote spatial modulations in the density profile, qualitatively agreeing with 3D Monte Carlo results. The theory also provides tools to assess thermal effects for potential thermometry applications.
method: Bogoliubov theory and the Local Density Approximation (LDA) are employed to derive a finite-temperature Gross-Pitaevskii equation (TeGPE).
model_or_system: The system consists of fully polarized dipoles trapped harmonically along the z-axis and in a box trap in the x-y plane. The interactions include contact and strong dipole-dipole interaction (DDI), making it relevant for dipolar supersolid stripes.
key_observables: Total column density, condensate fraction ($f_c$), spatial modulations/stripes, and thermal density profiles ($n_{th}$).
important_parameters: Tilting angle ($\alpha$), particle number ($N$), scattering length ($a$), and temperature ($T$). The strongly dipolar regime is key ($a \ll a_{dd}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis restricts itself to the limit of large condensate fractions ($f_c \geq 0.6$) and assumes the applicability of Bogoliubov theory, which breaks down in infinite 2D systems.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate density changes by varying particle number or scattering length at zero T; they also compare 2D densities at $T=0$ versus finite $T$, showing temperature-induced modulations.
paper_structure: The paper builds from the general framework (Bogoliubov theory) to derive the TeGPE incorporating thermal fluctuations via LDA. It then analyzes structural changes by varying key parameters ($N, \alpha, T$) and concludes with applications for thermometry.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses complex many-body physics in ultracold atomic systems (dipolar BECs) by extending mean-field theory to include thermal fluctuations in a quasi-2D geometry, which is crucial for understanding exotic phases like supersolids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Contributions of the different axial excitation modes to the density functionals Ωth

FIG. 2. Integrated 2D density distribution (n = R dz|ψ(r)|2) of the dipolar system in a box trap at zero temperature. In each row, one parameter is varied while the rest are fixed to the values a/add = 0.7, α = 77.35o, N = 19935. The box trap has widths Lx/r0 = Ly/r0 = 41.02 while the axial harmonic trap is set to ℏω/E0 = 1.

FIG. 3. Integrated 2D density distribution (n = R dz|ψ(r)|2) of the dipolar system for box traps with different aspect ratios at zero temperature. Each column and row correspond to a different particle number and box trap, respectively. In each column, the average 2D density is kept fixed. The widths along the y-axis of each box trap are given by Ly/r0 = 41.02 for Box 1, Ly/r0 = 82.04 for Box 2 and Ly/r0 = 27.36 for Box 3. The rest of the parameters of the calculations are a/add = 0.7, α = 77.35o and ℏω/E0 = 1.

FIG. 4. Energy per particle as a function of N for the tilted dipolar system in three different box traps. The dashed- dotted lines correspond to the results of the interacting system while the solid lines correspond to the trapped ideal gas. The parameters of the calculations are the same as in Fig. 3.
FIG. 6. Total column density n(y) = R dxdz |ψ(r)|2 + R dx nth(r⊥) at zero and finite temperature for N = 25000 (top) and N = 40000 (bottom). The rest of the parameters are the same as in Fig. 5.
